item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Full Attribution:

1. First image component owned by MrX under CC BY-SA 3.0.
2. Second image component owned by Photojack50 under CC BY-SA 4.0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a stylistic edit. Please ask the author for permission before making such edits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Changed the date in the addendum to be day/month/year. Hope you don't mind! (If it was a stylistic choice feel free to revert)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for catching that! I just quickly went through my articles before and added them. I've since fixed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, noticed an error relating to the anomaly bar. Kenneq and Notice are in the wrong places. Notice is for risk class. (Green, low.) While Kenneq would be for the disruption class. Mind if I fix it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really love this, and I am generally not a fan of mixing my funny with my spooky. Multiple points in the article made my put my hand to my mouth with a "what the hell?" reaction (in a good way, I assure you. Partly humored, partly disturbed). Those moments were…
-"SCP-4286 causes bones within the human body to resemble various household objects"
-"an animate human skeleton"
-"proceeds to push the femur into the hat"
-"produces a claw hammer "
-"produces an extended pocket knife, a socket wrench, and a length of black cord"
All in all, great execution of a goofy, disturbing, sweet story.

item.title: Re: Good Job
item.content:encoded: I agree. It makes less sense in-universe, but it reads much better this way, so it definitely belongs in the log.

item.title: Re: quirky
item.content:encoded: Huh, okay. So this thing can affect mass amounts of people without anyone realizing it. Neato.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, glad you liked it. I was told from one of my other critter that there’s a fine line between a friendly skeleton doctor and a straight up bone thief, so I decided to apply wholesomeness without restraint.

There’s not too much I can do about the little effects list, but I made the strength of the bones not have an exact strength, and changed a sentence or two. I also just made the -1 pop out of the closet, since the black smoke was a leftover from a previous version of the draft.

Thanks for the crit!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dammit I was literally typing up crit for this draft in a PM when I thought to check for you in IRC, and I saw this was posted. Sorry homie, I'll hit you back quicker next time.

Buuuuuuuut, I'm also on Site Crit, so I guess I can do this instead!

+ Nitpicks
- Nitpicks

These bones will seamlessly integrate themselves into the body's skeletal system. SCP-4286 affected bones will produce blood cells at a rate consistent with standard human bones. SCP-4286 affected individuals have shown a notable resistance to disease, with most individuals being completely cured of their previous skeletal ailment two months after their SCP-4286 occurrence. SCP-4286 affected bones have shown remarkable strength, with instances withstanding more than 5000 newtons of force without damaging. All attempts to surgically implant instances of SCP-4286 affected bones have resulted in rejection from the host body.

This bit here kind of feels almost like a "fast facts" section about the anomaly, with a lot of sentences starting in a similar manner (e.g. SCP-4286 affected [something]), leading to the section coming off as disjointed (heh puns).

Also, incredibly small nit to pick, but in what way can the instances withstand 5000 newtons of force? If I hit the top of a femur, it can take somewhere around 26700 newtons before breaking, but if I hit the side of it, 4000 newtons can do the trick. This is the difference between these bones barely being able to hold most average body weight versus being stronger than a regular bone.

SCP-4286-1 then reaches into its hat and produces a small bandage stylized with a cartoon heart. This bandage is removed from its packaging and adhered to the child's arm. SCP-4286-1 gathers the items that were removed from its hat placing the hat on its head. SCP-4286-1 then bends over the child, touching its mouth to the child's forehead in a kissing motion.

Gonna be honest, this right here is what makes the article. It's so wholesome, strange, and just generally WTF in the best kind of way. Really, the whole log is awesome.

00:02:14 - Black smoke rises from below the camera, obscuring the view of the room.

With the above praise, there is a minor caveat. Not a fan of the anomalous black smoke to obscure the camera feed. I really would have preferred if he appeared and disappeared via zany circumstances, like coming out of a toy chest and disappearing into the closet, for example. Overall a minor gripe, but the black smoke feels a bit too out of nowhere for this anomaly.

I wasn't so sure about where this was going by the end of the description, so the introduction of a skeleton in a party hat playing doctor and knocking over toys came totally out of left field in the best possible way. +1 from me.

item.title: Re: quirky
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the compliment! It’s Keter because people who experience 4286 don’t know it until they have a medical procedure, people affected by it will suddenly pop up once they have an X-Ray or similar. Also because people don’t know when they are affected by 4286, they don’t know when or where the -1 will appear, and can’t fully contain it. And it’s not like people aren’t going to get X-ray, because someone with Osteoporosis suddenly getting better isn’t going to be ignored.

item.title: quirky
item.content:encoded: I don't see how this is a keter class, but I like it anyway. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I think this might have crossed into -J territory if the birthday skeleton was just collecting bones for his big ol’ bone collection.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel this would’ve been a lot better as a -J for October, but a skeleton stealing bones is always epic, good job.

item.title: Re: Good Job
item.content:encoded: I feel like putting -1 in the description would lose some of the punch the video log has. Instead of providing more info on what causes SCP-4286, it would just feel a bit redundant.

item.title: Good Job
item.content:encoded: This skip was a very pleasant read and had its humuerus moments. However, I would recommend that you put scp-4286-1 in the description instead of just introducing him in the log.

item.title: Happy Anniversary
item.content:encoded:
CELEBRATION
spoopy celebration

Today is a special day my friends. Exactly 365 days ago (give or take a few hours) I first joined this website. 365 days of pianos, VHS tapes, giant squids, and posters. I just want to thank everyone on this site who has ever interacted with me in any way, positively or negatively, for making this website so near and dear to me.

Big thanks to my fantastic critters:

  • AthosNetwork for wading through my awful first two drafts and sticking with me.
  • T Rutherford for his fantastic critique on the end log.
  • rattles for his great critique on the formatting and description.
  • My personal friend Chrisse who helped me develop this idea in the first place.
  • and my honorary critter TheMightyMcB.

Thank you all for putting up with me for so long. Here's to another 365 days of dumb puns and weird ideas.

Other Articles of Mine
